_meta.notes: Ch3 Chapter Review. All of Chapter 3.

cards.body: Every food word you learned started as a root: kain, luto, inom. Add um- or mag- and it's a verb. Add pag- or -in and it's a noun. One root, many words. This is how Tagalog works.

cards.explanation: Roots transform with affixes: um-, mag-/nag-, pag-, -in.

cards.prompt: Lola asks if you've eaten yet.

cards.explanation: Nagluto (nag- = past) si Nanay ng sinigang.

cards.source_text: The adobo is amazing. Tell Lola:
cards.explanation: Masarap ang pagkain = the food is delicious!

cards.body: You can now chain desire + action: 'Gusto kong kumain ng adobo' = I want to eat adobo. Swap gusto for ayaw and it flips: 'Ayaw kong magluto' = I don't want to cook. One pattern, endless sentences.

cards.source_text: You're at a carinderia. You want to know the price.
Lola offers more food. You're full.
options: Busog na po ako, Lola. Salamat po!
cards.explanation: Busog na po ako = I'm full already (respectful). The polite Filipino decline.
Ayaw kong magluto = I don't want to cook.

cards.source_text: What's the difference between mag- and nag-?
cards.explanation: mag- = present/future (magluto = will cook). nag- = past (nagluto = cooked).

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: You just completed the Food chapter. You can now talk about eating, cooking, drinking, wanting, refusing, and ordering. That covers about 60% of daily Filipino conversation.
The verb patterns you learned (um- infix, mag-/nag- prefix) work with hundreds of roots. Every new root word you learn from now on immediately gives you multiple verbs.
'Kumain ka na ba?' went from a mysterious sentence in Chapter 1 to something you fully understand: K-um-ain (ate, um-infix) ka (you) na (already) ba (question). That's real progress.
You've learned ~71 words across three chapters. Research says 500 words covers 80% of daily conversation. You're 14% there.
